locked_facts: Pseudo force = -ma_frame (opposite to frame's acceleration, acts on all objects in that frame)
Pseudo forces are needed only in non-inertial (accelerating) frames
Lift going up: apparent weight W' = m(g + a) — you feel heavier
Lift going down: apparent weight W' = m(g - a) — you feel lighter
Centrifugal force = mω²r (outward in rotating frame) — pseudo force
Coriolis force = -2m(ω × v) — deflects moving objects in rotating frame
truth_statements: Pseudo forces do not arise from any physical interaction — they are a mathematical consequence of using a non-inertial reference frame
Newton's laws in their standard form apply only in inertial frames — in non-inertial frames pseudo forces must be added
In a freely falling lift the effective gravity is zero and objects appear weightless
The centrifugal force felt in a turning car is a pseudo force that appears only in the car's rotating reference frame
An observer in an inertial frame sees no pseudo forces — only real forces like gravity, tension, and friction

aha_moment.what_to_show: Person on a weighing scale in a lift. Ground frame: only real forces (gravity + normal). Lift frame: must add pseudo force -ma to make Newton's laws work. When lift accelerates up: pseudo force is downward → scale reads m(g+a). When in free fall: pseudo force = mg upward → scale reads zero (weightlessness).
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: Split screen: ground frame (real forces only) vs lift frame (real + pseudo) — both give the same physical result
aha_moment.why_this_breaks_misconception: Students think pseudo forces are real physical forces — seeing the same situation from two frames (one with, one without pseudo force) shows they're a frame-dependent mathematical tool

simulation_states.label: Inertial vs non-inertial frame
simulation_states.description: A ball in a car. Car brakes suddenly. Ground observer: ball continues forward (inertia). Car observer: ball 'pushed' forward by a pseudo force. No real force pushes the ball forward.
simulation_states.emphasis: The pseudo force exists only in the accelerating frame — it's not a real physical interaction

simulation_states.label: Lift problems
simulation_states.description: Person on scale in a lift. Lift at rest: W = mg. Accelerating up: W' = m(g+a). Accelerating down: W' = m(g-a). Free fall: W' = 0.
simulation_states.emphasis: Your apparent weight changes with the lift's acceleration — but your mass doesn't

simulation_states.label: Two-frame comparison
simulation_states.description: Same scenario shown from ground frame and lift frame simultaneously. Ground: normal + gravity → ma. Lift: normal + gravity + pseudo → 0 (equilibrium in lift frame). Both give same physical outcome.
simulation_states.emphasis: Two valid descriptions — pseudo forces are the price of using a non-inertial frame

simulation_states.label: Interactive
simulation_states.description: Student controls lift acceleration (up/down/free fall). Scale reading updates. Centrifugal force demo in rotating frame. Pendulum in accelerating train.
simulation_states.emphasis: Accelerate the lift — watch the scale change. Try free fall — weightlessness!

common_misconceptions.belief: Pseudo forces are real forces exerted by something
common_misconceptions.correction: Pseudo forces have no physical source — no object exerts them. They arise mathematically when you use Newton's laws in a non-inertial (accelerating) frame. In an inertial frame, the same motion is explained without any pseudo force.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show same scenario from inertial frame (no pseudo force) and non-inertial frame (with pseudo force) — same physical result

common_misconceptions.belief: Astronauts in ISS are beyond Earth's gravity
common_misconceptions.correction: At ISS altitude (~400 km), g ≈ 8.7 m/s² (only ~11% less than surface). Astronauts feel weightless because the ISS is in free fall (orbiting = continuously falling). The apparent weight is zero: W' = m(g - g) = 0.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show ISS orbiting with gravity arrow still pointing to Earth — free fall creates the sensation of weightlessness
